danger_signals.visual_form: Screen-space gaps and drops in level design
danger_signals.meaning: Instant death hazard; player cannot recover
Environmental kill zone; Big Nose cannot swim

danger_signals.visual_form: Mechanical and environmental hazards scattered throughout levels
danger_signals.meaning: Stage-specific threats requiring avoidance or item defense

danger_signals.visual_form: Animated hostile entities in play space
danger_signals.meaning: Combat threat; can be neutralized only by club-stone attack, not jump-on mechanic

danger_signals.visual_form: Big Nose sprite without stone upgrade visual indicator
danger_signals.meaning: One-hit death state; immediate danger from any enemy contact

safety_signals.visual_form: Static structures within islands where bones can be spent
safety_signals.meaning: Safe trading zones; player can purchase spells, ability upgrades, and protective items

safety_signals.visual_form: Visual equipment or status change on Big Nose sprite
safety_signals.meaning: Damage mitigation; grants one-hit protection; enables club-based projectile attacks

safety_signals.visual_form: Level segment boundaries (implied by level structure)
safety_signals.meaning: Mid-stage safe positions; however, losing all lives resets to level start
Currency for safety purchases; resource accumulation indicates progression toward defensive items

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Caveman protagonist with large nose sprite
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Prehistoric caveman aesthetic; club weapon as identity symbol

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Dinosaurs and hostile creatures implied by island names (Monster Island, Terror Island, Chaos Island)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Escalating threat designs across four islands: Paradise → Monster → Terror → Chaos
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Island theming communicates enemy composition; later islands indicate stronger, more diverse threats
Distributed across islands as safe trading points; neutral to player faction

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Relatively open, safe-looking environment with basic enemy types
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Lower enemy density and aggression; more forgiving platform layout

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Increased environmental hazard density; more complex enemy placements
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Escalating difficulty through trap placement and enemy variety; shop upgrades become critical

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Visual peak of hostile design; maximum environmental and enemy threat
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: All hazard types combined; no checkpoint system recovery if lives exhausted; final challenge

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Big Nose sprite in base state without protective equipment visual
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: One-hit death vulnerability; combat avoidance required; incentivizes shop visits

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Big Nose sprite with stone upgrade indication
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: One additional hit allowed; enables offensive projectile attack; increased risk tolerance in level navigation

environmental_storytelling.example: Four-island sequence (Paradise → Monster → Terror → Chaos) communicates narrative escalation and danger without explicit dialogue
Core mechanic framing ('Big Nose runs through islands to find his dinner because he is very hungry') establishes caveman survival narrative through hunger goal rather than written story
Pitfalls, lava, water, and traps increase in concentration across islands, implying each island is progressively more hostile and dinosaur-infested
Available shops suggest minimal civilization infrastructure in island chains; commerce exists but is scarce, reinforcing primitive setting
Dinosaur enemies (implied by 'videos about dinosaurs' category) populate Monster/Terror/Chaos islands, creating prehistoric fauna narrative without exposition

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: One-hit death; must avoid all enemy contact; incentivizes avoidance platforming
Can sustain one hit; can project club-stones as offensive projectiles (up to three stones); increased aggression viability
Can kill player on contact unless player has stone defense; require club-stone projectile attacks to defeat (not jump mechanic)
Static; accept bone currency; dispense spells, ability upgrades, and defensive items; signal safe interaction zones
Instant death on contact; no recovery mechanic; force level restart if player dies with no lives remaining

notes: Big Nose the Caveman uses minimal explicit signaling due to NES-era technical constraints. World-state communication relies heavily on level design progression (four-island sequence), enemy placement density escalation, and thematic naming rather than UI elements. The game's primary danger signal is the presence of hazards and enemies in the environment itself. Safety is communicated through shop access and the visual distinction between protected (stone-equipped) and unprotected player states. No faction conflict is present; instead, a solo-player-versus-environment dynamic dominates, with the world itself (dinosaurs, hazards) as the antagonist. The removed 'Help' balloon and rescue mechanics (visible in unused graphics) suggest an earlier design iteration that may have included NPC factions, which were ultimately cut. The caveman protagonist's large nose is his primary visual identity marker and directly references his name—a simple but effective character branding in 8-bit context.
